15. Modeling as Inference

15.1. Introduction

This lecture is presents a NonBayesian perspective of L13. Recall L13-L15 is a series of 3 connected lectures:

Supose you don’t have the correct model for potential data but you know it lies in a class parameterized by :

(15.1)

Modeling goal: Want to make inferences about . Don’t care about per-se, only our ability to model . E.g.

  • Language modeling: Predict

  • Image classification: where denotes image and denotes class label, and we want to compute

    (15.2)

  • We measure some i.i.d. Bernoulli variables for some bias , and we want to best characterize the uncertainty in .

    • One approach: Construct the distribution according to MLE estimator for can be justified asymptotically but is not the best approach for any finite value of .

serves as a natural measure of “goodness” of . (recall above discussion that the reverse KL divergence is mode-seeking)

We develop a fundamental approach for such modeling problems. Interestingly, we neither require nor benefit from explicitly estimating .

15.2. Modeling via Mixtures

The key to our approach is to model as a mixture of the class18

(15.3)

where are all nonnegative and sum to one. From a Bayesian viewpoint, would be interpreted as a prior over , since . We will return to this.

Plan: We will first discuss a framework to optimize and then show that it is useful for inference.

Remark. The approach where we define the set of distributions based on take convex combinations in parameter space as opposed to the distributions themselves is worse and less tractable.

15.3. [REVIEW] Optimization Framework

Now we know it is sufficient to optimize a mixture. A natural means for choosing is according to a minimax criterion. I.e. we choose to try to make the divergence as small as possible, knowing that nature will subsequently choose to opp on us. We define the minimax redunancy in 19.

(15.6)

Lemma 15.1 (Relaxation turns nonconvex optimization into convex optimization).

(15.7)

This is kinda trivial and holds in general because:

(15.8)

Hence we have

(15.9)

and the interpretation is that you first pick a distribution and nature picks some weights to opp on us.

From this form, we apply the following saddle-point theorem, which establishes the equivalence between minimax and maximin redundancy. It turns out the maximin version is easier to perform directly!

The quantities involved above have names:

Definition 15.1 (Least informative prior and Capacity). For discrete , consider the family of models

(15.16)

  • The least informative prior for is given by

(15.17)

  • The capacity of the model family is the (average) cost reduction (where is the cost function) associated with the least informative prior, i.e.

(15.18)

Remarks

  • The model capacity expresses the richness of the model family (expressive power). There is a tradeoff: Larger classes of models are more expressive but come with larger values of (nature can force a larger loss because the family is harder to approximate uniformly).

  • For , the optimum model satisfies the equidistance property (super intuitive geometrically, but is Theorem 15.3 in class).

  • Recall .

    1. If can be determined uniquely from , then the latter term is zero. So (nature picks a uniform distribution).
    2. If no information about can be determined from , then .

  • When we have a sequence of observations each from , optimizing weights depends on , which is sometimes referred to as the horizon of the problem.

15.4. Computation of Model Capacity

Recall KL divergence is convex.

Proposition 15.1 (Concavity of entropy). For finite , the entropy is concave in .
Proof

□

Note that we can alternatively denote the mutual information as .

Proposition 15.2 (Concavity of mutual information). is concave in and convex in .

Hence the optimization problem is concave, and so the problem is well behaved.

15.5. [REVIEW] Inference with Mixture Models

Okay so we know how to find the distribution that minimizes KL divergence within a model class. How can we do inference (i.e. infer new given old observed )? One thing I thought of was to update each distribution in the model class from to but this requires a new capacity optimization for every observed 20. Instead, we compute the minimax distribution and apply conditional probability on it:

How do we use ? In a general scenario, there are a set of variables that we observe and a set that we do not observe but about which we want to make inferences (e.g. future from past in time series data).

Specifically, we want to find some that is close to the true conditional

Note that we computed where and then we compute its marginal to substitute into 15.31.

This can be seen of as a weighing-by-relative likelihood “soft-decision” approach while ML likelihood is a kind of “hard-decision” modeling

Remark. It turns out that in many cases, modeling performance is not too sensitive to the exact details of . Often it is suficient to just choose a weighting that includes all models, i.e. , an example of which is the uniform weighting. Such a uniform prior is referred to as maximally ignorant.

Example 15.1 (What is the probability the sun will rise tomorrow?). Let for some unknown . Given observations predict outcome of day . Weighting with a uniform prior turns out to effectively produce an estimate of where is the number of ones. The MLE approach yields the estimate which is less appealing for small values of n.

The mixture modeling approach happens to degenerate to an instance of estimate-and-substitute. But this doesn’t happen in general.
